name: Bounded Polymorphism

laws: A type parameter is restricted to types satisfying given constraints
Only operations guaranteed by the bound may be used on the parameter
Bounds are checked at the definition, not each instantiation (in checked generics)
x.encoding: Rust trait bounds / where, C++20 concepts, C# generic constraints (where T:), Java bounded type parameters (<T extends ...>). C uses _Generic / conventions.

def: Parametric polymorphism whose type parameters are constrained to types satisfying specified interfaces/traits, so generic code can use those capabilities safely.
problem: Unconstrained generics can only treat values opaquely; you need to require operations (ordering, hashing, display) on the parameter.
mechanism: A bound (trait/concept/constraint/extends) restricts the parameter; inside the generic, only the bound’s operations are available. Checked-generic languages verify the body once against the bound, catching errors before instantiation.
benefits: Use parameter capabilities while staying generic
failures: Under-constraining and leaning on templates’ late errors (C++ without concepts)
myths: "Generics are always unbounded" — bounds are what make them useful beyond containers
